text: The surgeon is performing a procedure to address a colon stricture, likely involving the visualization and manipulation of the affected colon segment.
The video shows the dilated proximal bowel, indicating a significant expansion of the bowel segment near the colon stricture.
The surgeon prepares to decompress the visibly dilated proximal bowel in the area planned for resection.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 silk suture, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a purse string suture on the bowel, preparing to decompress the visibly dilated proximal bowel.
The surgeon observes the colon filled with contents, indicating that nothing was able to pass through the bowel.
The surgeon ties off the colotomy, securing the incision made in the colon.
The surgeon suspends the uterus to gain access for the upcoming resection.
The surgeon observes the adherence of the colon to the left adnexa, indicating a close attachment between these structures.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the colon using a medial to lateral approach, carefully separating the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins dissection just underneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery over the sacral promontory, preparing for further surgical steps.
The surgeon identifies the left colic artery branching from the superior hemorrhoidal artery, planning to preserve it during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the superior hemorrhoidal artery, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs medial dissection near the superior hemorrhoidal artery, focusing on the area over the sacral promontory.
The surgeon proceeds with lateral dissection, moving towards the lateral aspect of the colon.
The surgeon observes a very large and redundant colon, indicating that extensive lateral dissection and mobilization of the flexure were not necessary.
The surgeon carefully dissects and separates the adherence of the colon from the left adnexa, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection of the colon, ensuring it is fully mobilized from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon proceeds with a standard sigmoid resection after completing the dissection of the colon from the left adnexa.
The surgeon examines the planned distal margin and assesses the caliber of the bowel, ensuring appropriate resection length and bowel condition.
The surgeon performs additional rectal dissection to facilitate the elevation of the rectum for anastomosis and to transect the rectal mesentery, revealing the tissue layers involved.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect and transect the proximal mesentery, preparing for the next step in the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a 60mm green stapler to transect the chronically dilated bowel, demonstrating the extent of dilation visually.
The surgeon uses two sixty millimeter staplers to transect the bowel, indicating the extent of its chronic dilation.
On the rectal side, a single stapler load is applied smoothly across the tissue, indicating ease of stapling in this area.
The surgeon begins the sewn anastomosis, connecting the bowel segments using sutures.
The surgeon prepares to perform a two-layer anastomosis, indicating the chosen technique for connecting the bowel segments.
The surgeon uses two OPDS sutures to create the outer row of the anastomosis, securing the bowel edges together.
The surgeon begins suturing at the confluence of the two staple lines on the proximal colon side to address the size discrepancy between the sections.
The surgeon offsets the proximal colon to the patient's left and proceeds to sew a two-layer end-to-end anastomosis, aligning the tissue for optimal connection.
The surgeon switches to using a 2-0 PDS suture for the outer layer of the anastomosis, replacing the previously considered 3-0 V-lock suture.
The surgeon completes the outer back row of sutures in the two-layer end-to-end anastomosis, securing the proximal colon in its offset position.
The surgeon proceeds to make collatomies, creating openings in the colon tissue as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon checks the inner lip of the anastomosis using indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence to assess perfusion.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Loc suture to secure the inner row of the anastomosis.
The surgeon places a specimen in the surgical field and prepares to start the next suture at the posterior midline.
The surgeon uses two PDS sutures to perform the outer anterior row of the anastomosis.
The surgeon ties the anterior PDS suture to the posterior PDS suture, completing a two-layer anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the completed two-layer anastomosis, visually inspecting for any signs of fluid leakage.
The scope is positioned directly at the anastomosis site, revealing a widely patent connection.
The surgical field is observed with no visible bleeding and no bubbles present, indicating a successful leak test.
The surgeon cleans up the sutures around the anastomosis site, ensuring a tidy surgical field.
The surgeon prepares the resected colon specimen for extraction from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon repositions the uterus back into its anatomical location.
